exercises.title: HTML Tag Parser
exercises.description: Parse HTML strings manually without BeautifulSoup (practicing the underlying concepts). Write `find_all_tags(html, tag)` that finds all occurrences of a tag and returns their text content and attributes. Write `find_links(html)` that extracts all `<a>` tags with their href and text. Test on sample HTML.
exercises.starterCode:
import re def find_all_tags(html, tag): """ Find all occurrences of ... and return list of text content. Simple version — handles non-nested tags. """ # TODO: Use re.findall with a pattern like r']*>(.*?)' # re.DOTALL makes '.' match newlines too pass def find_links(html): """ Extract all text links. Returns list of {'href': ..., 'text': ...} dicts. """ # TODO: Use regex to find href attribute and link text # Pattern: text pass def find_by_class(html, tag, css_class): """ Find elements with a specific CSS class. Returns list of text content. """ # TODO: Find ... elements pass html = """

Book Store

Python Crash Course

$29.99

Eric Matthes

View Details

Fluent Python

$49.99

Luciano Ramalho

View Details
Home About Us """ # Find all h2 tags titles = find_all_tags(html, "h2") print("Book titles:") for title in titles: print(f" {title}") # Find all links links = find_links(html) print("\nLinks:") for link in links: print(f" {link['text']}: {link['href']}") # Find prices by class prices = find_by_class(html, "p", "price") print("\nPrices:") for price in prices: print(f" {price}")
exercises.expectedOutput:
Book titles:
  Python Crash Course
  Fluent Python

Links:
  View Details: /books/1
  View Details: /books/2
  Home: /home
  About Us: /about

Prices:
  $29.99
  $49.99
hints: find_links: find href with re.findall(r']+href="([^"]+)"[^>]*>(.*?)', html, re.DOTALL)
Strip whitespace from text content: text.strip()

exercises.title: Structured Data Extractor
exercises.description: Extract structured data from a simulated HTML product listing page. Parse multiple products from HTML, extract their fields (name, price, rating, availability), and produce a clean list of dicts. Then filter and sort the results.
exercises.starterCode:
import re PRODUCT_HTML = """
Python Handbook 29.99 4.5 In Stock
Data Science Guide 44.99 4.2 Out of Stock
Flask Web Dev 34.99 4.8 In Stock
Clean Code 39.99 4.7 In Stock
""" def extract_products(html): """ Parse all products from the HTML. Returns list of dicts with: id (int), name (str), price (float), rating (float), in_stock (bool) """ products = [] # TODO: Find each product block (div with class="product") # TODO: For each block, extract all span values by class # TODO: Parse price as float, rating as float, in_stock as bool ("In Stock" -> True) pass products = extract_products(PRODUCT_HTML) print(f"Found {len(products)} products") print() # Filter: in-stock only, sorted by rating desc in_stock = [p for p in products if p['in_stock']] in_stock_sorted = sorted(in_stock, key=lambda p: -p['rating']) print("In-stock books by rating:") for p in in_stock_sorted: print(f" [{p['rating']}] {p['name']} - ${p['price']}")
exercises.expectedOutput:
Found 4 products

In-stock books by rating:
  [4.8] Flask Web Dev - $34.99
  [4.7] Clean Code - $39.99
  [4.5] Python Handbook - $29.99
hints:
Find product blocks with: re.findall(r'
', html, re.DOTALL)
For each block, find span values: re.search(r'(.*?)', block)

exercises.title: Pagination Scraper Simulation
exercises.description: Simulate scraping multiple pages of results. Write a `scrape_all_pages(get_page_func, max_pages=5)` function that calls `get_page_func(page_num)` repeatedly, collecting results until either the function returns an empty list or max_pages is reached. Implement a polite scraper with rate tracking.
exercises.starterCode:
import time

# Simulated 'website' with paginated data
ALL_PRODUCTS = [
    f"Product {i}" for i in range(1, 18)  # 17 products total
]

def get_page(page_num, per_page=5):
    """Simulate fetching a page. Returns list of items or [] when exhausted."""
    start = (page_num - 1) * per_page
    end = start + per_page
    return ALL_PRODUCTS[start:end]  # Returns [] when start >= len(ALL_PRODUCTS)

def scrape_all_pages(get_page_func, max_pages=10, per_page=5):
    """
    Scrape all pages using get_page_func(page_num).
    Stops when empty page returned or max_pages reached.
    Returns (all_items, pages_fetched, total_requests).
    """
    all_items = []
    pages_fetched = 0

    for page in range(1, max_pages + 1):
        # TODO: Call get_page_func(page)
        # TODO: If empty, break (no more pages)
        # TODO: Add items to all_items
        # TODO: Increment pages_fetched
        pass

    return all_items, pages_fetched

all_products, pages = scrape_all_pages(get_page, max_pages=10, per_page=5)

print(f"Scraping complete:")
print(f"  Pages fetched: {pages}")
print(f"  Total products: {len(all_products)}")
print()
print("All products:")
for i, product in enumerate(all_products, 1):
    print(f"  {i:2}. {product}")
exercises.expectedOutput:
Scraping complete:
  Pages fetched: 4
  Total products: 17

All products:
   1. Product 1
   2. Product 2
   3. Product 3
   4. Product 4
   5. Product 5
   6. Product 6
   7. Product 7
   8. Product 8
   9. Product 9
  10. Product 10
  11. Product 11
  12. Product 12
  13. Product 13
  14. Product 14
  15. Product 15
  16. Product 16
  17. Product 17
hints: items = get_page_func(page); if not items: break
all_items.extend(items) to add all items from the page.
With 17 items and 5 per page: pages 1(5), 2(5), 3(5), 4(2), 5(empty) → 4 pages fetched.

warmup.title: Quick Recall: re.findall
warmup.starterCode: import re html = "hello and world and test" bold = re.findall(r'(.*?)', html) print(bold) print(len(bold)) print(bold[0].upper())
